mission.title: Install and Configure Ingress NGINX Controller
mission.description: Ingress NGINX Controller is a Kubernetes Ingress controller that uses NGINX as a reverse proxy and load balancer. It is suitable for managing external access to services in a Kubernetes cluster. However, it is important to note that the project is retiring, and users are encouraged to migrate to a Gateway API implementation.

steps.title: Add the Ingress NGINX Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Ingress NGINX Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add ingress-nginx https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/helm-chart/ingress-nginx

```

> This is the official repository for the Ingress NGINX Helm chart.

steps.title: Install the Ingress NGINX Controller
steps.description:
This command installs the Ingress NGINX Controller using Helm in the 'ingress-nginx' namespace.

```bash

helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace --version 4.10.1

```

> Make sure to specify the correct version of the Helm chart.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This command checks if the Ingress NGINX Controller pods are running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> You should see the Ingress NGINX pods in a Running state.

steps.title: Create an example Ingress resource
steps.description:
This step creates a sample Ingress resource to test the Ingress NGINX Controller.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: example-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

```

> Replace 'example.local' and 'example-service' with your actual service details.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Ingress NGINX Controller
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Ingress NGINX Controller and may result in data loss for associated resources.

```bash

helm uninstall ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx

```

> This will remove the Ingress NGINX resources, but ensure to check for orphaned resources.

uninstall.title: Delete the Ingress NGINX namespace
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the namespace where Ingress NGINX was installed, which may result in loss of all resources in that namespace.

```bash

kubectl delete namespace ingress-nginx

```

> Be cautious as this will delete all resources in the ingress-nginx namespace.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Ingress NGINX Controller
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure to back up your Ingress resources and configurations.

```bash

kubectl get ingress --all-namespaces -o yaml > ingress-backup.yaml

helm upgrade ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx --namespace ingress-nginx --version 4.10.1

kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

```

> Backup your existing Ingress resources.
> Upgrade to the specified version of the Helm chart.
> Verify that the new pods are running successfully after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error from server (InternalError): error when creating "example-ingress.yaml": Internal error occurred'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the Ingress resource.

**Fix:**
Check the Ingress resource configuration for errors and ensure the backend service is correctly defined.

troubleshooting.title: Ingress NGINX Controller pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to resource constraints or misconfigurations in the NGINX settings.

**Fix:**
Inspect the logs of the pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n ingress-nginx' and adjust resource requests/limits if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Ingress rules are not being applied.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Ingress NGINX Controller is not properly installed or running.

**Fix:**
Ensure the Ingress NGINX Controller pods are running and check the service configuration.

troubleshooting.title: 503 Service Unavailable error when accessing the Ingress.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the backend service is not reachable or not running.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the backend service and ensure it is running and accessible.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new annotations for custom NGINX configurations.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy annotations related to SSL termination.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update your Ingress resources to remove deprecated annotations.

resolution.summary: You should see the Ingress NGINX Controller pods running in the 'Running' state when you execute 'kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
